Behind the Paycheck: Inside DC's Public Workforce¶

A Compensation, Tenure, and Structure Analysis of the District's 35,000+ Public Employees¶

Author: Sule
Course: MATH 014 — Introduction to Data Science
Term: Spring 2026 · Howard University

1 · Introduction¶

The District of Columbia government employs more than forty thousand people across roughly ninety agencies — a workforce larger than many Fortune 500 companies and one whose payroll is funded entirely by public dollars. The DC Public Employee Salary dataset, published openly by DC Open Data, makes that payroll inspectable: every full-time employee, their job title, the agency that employs them, their pay grade, their hire date, and their annual compensation rate.

This project investigates three questions about that workforce:

  1. How does compensation vary across DC's agencies, and which agencies have the highest median pay?
  2. Does tenure predict pay — and does that relationship behave differently across appointment categories (Career Service, Educational Service, Management Supervisory Service, etc.)?
  3. Where is pay dispersion (inequality) most extreme inside individual agencies?

The dataset is analytically compelling because it sits at the intersection of three things this course has emphasized: real-world messiness (mixed casing, sentinel values, parseable date strings), mixed variable types (categorical agency codes, ordinal grades, continuous compensation, datetime hire dates), and narrative-rich groupings that reward both univariate and multivariate exploration.

2 · Importing Libraries¶

In [1]:
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import plotly.express as px

# Display settings
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", 30)
pd.set_option("display.width", 140)
sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid", context="notebook")
PALETTE = ["#0B5394", "#3D85C6", "#76A5AF", "#E69138", "#CC4125", "#674EA7"]
sns.set_palette(PALETTE)
plt.rcParams["figure.dpi"] = 100

3 · Data Loading¶

The raw CSV ships from DC Open Data with nine columns covering identity, role, agency, grade, compensation, hire date, and appointment type. We read it without any assumptions about types so we can inspect them directly.

In [2]:
raw = pd.read_csv("dc_salary_raw.csv")
print(f"Rows: {len(raw):,}   Columns: {raw.shape[1]}")
raw.head()

Rows: 40,611   Columns: 9
Out[2]:
FIRST_NAME LAST_NAME JOBTITLE DESCRSHORT GRADE COMPRATE HIREDATE_STRING GVT_TYPE_OF_APPT OBJECTID
0 Natasha Boley Compliance Specialist DBH 13 123873.00 2004/10/18 CS - Reg 1
1 Korlu Wolobah-Kuyon PSYCHIATRIC NURSE DBH 09 115907.00 2020/08/17 CS - Reg 2
2 Hadizat Elayo-Ross SUPERVISORY PSYCHIATRIC NURSE DBH 13 122500.00 2023/08/14 MSS - Reg 3
3 Gary Evans Climate Control Maint Mechanic DBH 11 41.73 1985/08/27 CS - Reg 4
4 Jemia Gross Special Police Officer DBH 08 75787.00 2016/11/14 CS - Reg 5

4 · Data Understanding¶

Before any cleaning we audit what we have: types, missingness, cardinality, and the shape of the numeric variable that will drive most of the analysis.

In [3]:
raw.dtypes
Out[3]:
FIRST_NAME              str
LAST_NAME               str
JOBTITLE                str
DESCRSHORT              str
GRADE                   str
COMPRATE            float64
HIREDATE_STRING         str
GVT_TYPE_OF_APPT        str
OBJECTID              int64
dtype: object
In [4]:
raw.isna().sum()
Out[4]:
FIRST_NAME           0
LAST_NAME            0
JOBTITLE             0
DESCRSHORT           0
GRADE               40
COMPRATE             0
HIREDATE_STRING      0
GVT_TYPE_OF_APPT     0
OBJECTID             0
dtype: int64
In [5]:
raw.nunique().sort_values(ascending=False)
Out[5]:
OBJECTID            40611
LAST_NAME           15110
FIRST_NAME          12930
HIREDATE_STRING      4914
COMPRATE             4360
JOBTITLE             3698
DESCRSHORT             88
GRADE                  68
GVT_TYPE_OF_APPT       18
dtype: int64
In [6]:
raw["COMPRATE"].describe().round(2)
Out[6]:
count     40611.00
mean      89087.76
std       47453.79
min           0.03
25%       66130.00
50%       93234.00
75%      119643.13
max      390232.00
Name: COMPRATE, dtype: float64

Initial read of the raw data:

  • 40,611 rows of employees, each with a unique OBJECTID, so there are no exact duplicates.
  • GRADE has 40 missing values out of ~40K rows — small enough to handle individually rather than drop.
  • COMPRATE has a minimum value of $0.03 and a maximum of $390,232. The lower end is clearly a data-entry artifact (no DC employee earns three cents annually) — we'll handle it with a salary floor during cleaning.
  • JOBTITLE is high-cardinality (3,698 distinct titles) — useful for spot-checks but not directly groupable.
  • DESCRSHORT (88 unique values) is the agency code — the right granularity for cross-group analysis.
  • GVT_TYPE_OF_APPT decomposes naturally into a category (CS, Ed, MSS, ExS, LS, Exe, Acting) and a status (Reg, Term, Temp). We'll engineer that split in the cleaning step.

5 · Data Cleaning¶

Cleaning is documented step-by-step below. Each transformation is justified inline; we re-check shape and missingness after the major operations.

In [7]:
df = raw.copy()

# 5a. Strip whitespace from all string columns
for c in df.select_dtypes(include="object").columns:
    df[c] = df[c].astype(str).str.strip()

# 5b. JOBTITLE arrives in mixed casing ("Compliance Specialist" vs "PSYCHIATRIC NURSE")
#     — title case standardizes display without loss of information.
df["JOBTITLE"] = df["JOBTITLE"].str.title()

# 5c. Parse HIREDATE_STRING ('YYYY/MM/DD') into a real datetime object
df["HIRE_DATE"] = pd.to_datetime(df["HIREDATE_STRING"], format="%Y/%m/%d", errors="coerce")
print(f"HIRE_DATE failed parses: {df['HIRE_DATE'].isna().sum()}")
df.head(3)

HIRE_DATE failed parses: 0
Out[7]:
FIRST_NAME LAST_NAME JOBTITLE DESCRSHORT GRADE COMPRATE HIREDATE_STRING GVT_TYPE_OF_APPT OBJECTID HIRE_DATE
0 Natasha Boley Compliance Specialist DBH 13 123873.0 2004/10/18 CS - Reg 1 2004-10-18
1 Korlu Wolobah-Kuyon Psychiatric Nurse DBH 09 115907.0 2020/08/17 CS - Reg 2 2020-08-17
2 Hadizat Elayo-Ross Supervisory Psychiatric Nurse DBH 13 122500.0 2023/08/14 MSS - Reg 3 2023-08-14
In [8]:
# 5d. Salary floor: drop rows with COMPRATE < $10,000.
# The raw minimum is $0.03 and other suspiciously-low values cluster below $5K.
# A $10K floor preserves part-time / seasonal positions while removing data-entry errors.
salary_floor = 10_000
n_dropped = (df["COMPRATE"] < salary_floor).sum()
print(f"Dropping {n_dropped} rows below ${salary_floor:,}")
df = df[df["COMPRATE"] >= salary_floor].copy()

Dropping 4984 rows below $10,000
In [9]:
# 5e. Fill missing GRADE with 'UNK' rather than dropping
#     (those 40 rows still carry information about pay/agency/tenure)
df["GRADE"] = df["GRADE"].fillna("UNK").replace({"nan": "UNK"})

# 5f. Engineer features
ANALYSIS_DATE = pd.Timestamp("2026-04-26")
df["TENURE_YEARS"] = ((ANALYSIS_DATE - df["HIRE_DATE"]).dt.days / 365.25).round(2)
df["HIRE_YEAR"] = df["HIRE_DATE"].dt.year
df["HIRE_DECADE"] = (df["HIRE_YEAR"] // 10 * 10).astype("Int64").astype(str) + "s"

# Decompose 'CS - Reg' into category + status for richer grouping
appt = df["GVT_TYPE_OF_APPT"].str.split(" - ", n=1, expand=True)
df["APPT_CATEGORY"] = appt[0].str.strip()
df["APPT_STATUS"] = appt[1].fillna("Other").str.strip()

# 5g. Drop the now-redundant raw columns
df = df.drop(columns=["OBJECTID", "HIREDATE_STRING"])

print(f"Cleaned shape: {df.shape}")
df.head(3)

Cleaned shape: (35627, 13)
Out[9]:
FIRST_NAME LAST_NAME JOBTITLE DESCRSHORT GRADE COMPRATE GVT_TYPE_OF_APPT HIRE_DATE TENURE_YEARS HIRE_YEAR HIRE_DECADE APPT_CATEGORY APPT_STATUS
0 Natasha Boley Compliance Specialist DBH 13 123873.0 CS - Reg 2004-10-18 21.52 2004 2000s CS Reg
1 Korlu Wolobah-Kuyon Psychiatric Nurse DBH 09 115907.0 CS - Reg 2020-08-17 5.69 2020 2020s CS Reg
2 Hadizat Elayo-Ross Supervisory Psychiatric Nurse DBH 13 122500.0 MSS - Reg 2023-08-14 2.70 2023 2020s MSS Reg
In [10]:
# 5h. Verify post-clean state: no missingness in critical columns, types correct
df.isna().sum()
Out[10]:
FIRST_NAME          0
LAST_NAME           0
JOBTITLE            0
DESCRSHORT          0
GRADE               0
COMPRATE            0
GVT_TYPE_OF_APPT    0
HIRE_DATE           0
TENURE_YEARS        0
HIRE_YEAR           0
HIRE_DECADE         0
APPT_CATEGORY       0
APPT_STATUS         0
dtype: int64
In [11]:
df.dtypes
Out[11]:
FIRST_NAME                     str
LAST_NAME                      str
JOBTITLE                       str
DESCRSHORT                     str
GRADE                          str
COMPRATE                   float64
GVT_TYPE_OF_APPT               str
HIRE_DATE           datetime64[us]
TENURE_YEARS               float64
HIRE_YEAR                    int32
HIRE_DECADE                    str
APPT_CATEGORY                  str
APPT_STATUS                    str
dtype: object
In [12]:
# Persist the cleaned dataset (Canvas deliverable)
df.to_csv("sule_final_project.csv", index=False)
print("Saved cleaned dataset → sule_final_project.csv")

Saved cleaned dataset → sule_final_project.csv

6 · Exploratory Data Analysis¶

With clean data in hand we build a quantitative picture of compensation, agency size, and appointment structure before any plotting.

In [13]:
# Compensation percentiles
df["COMPRATE"].quantile([0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 0.90, 0.95, 0.99]).round(0)
Out[13]:
0.10     59736.0
0.25     75433.0
0.50    100313.0
0.75    123873.0
0.90    140000.0
0.95    160000.0
0.99    206802.0
Name: COMPRATE, dtype: float64

The compensation distribution is fairly tight by federal-payroll standards: 80% of employees earn between roughly $60K and $140K, with a long right tail extending to nearly $400K for senior executives.

In [14]:
# Appointment-category breakdown — median pay and headcount
(df.groupby("APPT_CATEGORY")
    .agg(headcount=("COMPRATE", "size"),
         median_pay=("COMPRATE", "median"),
         mean_pay=("COMPRATE", "mean"))
    .sort_values("median_pay", ascending=False)
    .round(0))
Out[14]:
headcount median_pay mean_pay
APPT_CATEGORY
Exe 68 226596.0 205241.0
LS 671 154877.0 154601.0
MSS 2552 125000.0 127746.0
ExS 599 120824.0 128488.0
Acting 9 111419.0 102457.0
Ed 10914 103780.0 99714.0
CS 20814 94139.0 96466.0

The appointment category is the single strongest predictor of pay in this dataset: Executive (Exe) roles cluster around $225K medians; Career Service (CS) — the largest category at ~20K employees — sits near $95K.

In [15]:
# Top 15 agencies by employee count, with their median pay
top15 = df["DESCRSHORT"].value_counts().head(15).index
(df[df["DESCRSHORT"].isin(top15)]
   .groupby("DESCRSHORT")
   .agg(employees=("COMPRATE", "size"),
        median_pay=("COMPRATE", "median"),
        mean_tenure=("TENURE_YEARS", "mean"))
   .sort_values("employees", ascending=False)
   .round(1))
Out[15]:
employees median_pay mean_tenure
DESCRSHORT
DCPS 10133 105071.0 9.5
MPD 4095 110595.0 12.6
FEMS 2335 92113.0 13.2
OCFO 1386 118558.0 14.4
DHS 1178 89329.0 13.9
DBH 1166 101290.0 13.9
DDOT 1135 83500.0 12.6
DOC 1066 73954.0 11.7
UDC 838 81206.2 11.8
DOH 801 101290.0 9.4
DOES 718 88300.0 11.4
OAG 674 118558.0 10.6
CFSA 654 107046.0 15.4
DPR 580 68116.0 14.1
DCPL 544 70919.0 13.0

DC Public Schools (DCPS) dominates the workforce at over 10,000 employees — roughly three out of every ten employees in the cleaned dataset. The Metropolitan Police Department (MPD) is the second-largest at ~4,100, and Fire & EMS (FEMS) third at ~2,300.

7 · Data Visualization and Interpretation¶

Seven core visualizations follow. Each is preceded by a statement of intent and followed by a written interpretation — a structure that ties every figure back to one of the three research questions.

7.1 · Univariate — Distribution of Annual Compensation¶

Intent: Establish the global shape of the response variable before we slice it. We overlay the median and mean to see how skewed the tail is.

In [16]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5.5))
sns.histplot(df["COMPRATE"], bins=60, kde=True, color=PALETTE[0],
             ax=ax, edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.4)
median_pay = df["COMPRATE"].median()
mean_pay = df["COMPRATE"].mean()
ax.axvline(median_pay, color=PALETTE[4], ls="--", lw=2, label=f"Median  ${median_pay:,.0f}")
ax.axvline(mean_pay, color=PALETTE[3], ls="--", lw=2, label=f"Mean    ${mean_pay:,.0f}")
ax.set_title("Distribution of Annual Compensation", fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("Annual Compensation (USD)")
ax.set_ylabel("Number of Employees")
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(plt.matplotlib.ticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"${x/1000:.0f}K"))
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Interpretation. The distribution is bimodal-ish, with a primary mode near $70K–$100K (the bulk of Career Service employees) and a secondary shoulder near $120K–$140K. The mean ($101.5K) and median ($100.3K) are nearly identical, which is unusual for a salary distribution and tells us that the upper tail — though it stretches to nearly $400K — contains too few employees to drag the mean meaningfully above the median. This is the empirical fingerprint of a capped compensation system with grade ceilings, not a winner-take-all market.

7.2 · Univariate — Largest Agencies by Headcount¶

Intent: Identify which agencies dominate the workforce so that downstream group-level analyses focus on agencies large enough to support stable estimates.

In [17]:
top15 = df["DESCRSHORT"].value_counts().head(15)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6.5))
bars = ax.barh(top15.index[::-1], top15.values[::-1], color=PALETTE[1], edgecolor="white")
bars[-1].set_color(PALETTE[0])
ax.set_title("The 15 Largest DC Agencies by Employee Count", fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("Number of Employees")
for bar, v in zip(bars, top15.values[::-1]):
    ax.text(bar.get_width() + 100, bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()/2,
            f"{v:,}", va="center", fontsize=10)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Interpretation. A handful of agencies absorb most of the workforce. DCPS alone is ~2.5× the size of the next-largest agency (MPD). Together, the top three (DCPS, MPD, FEMS) account for over 16,500 of the 35,627 cleaned employees — nearly half. This concentration matters for every subsequent comparison: medians and dispersion ratios for these three agencies are statistically robust, while smaller agencies will sometimes show noisy effects.

7.3 · Bivariate — Compensation by Appointment Category¶

Intent: Compare the seven appointment categories side-by-side to see which structural employment categories command the highest pay and which carry the most internal variability.

In [18]:
order = (df.groupby("APPT_CATEGORY")["COMPRATE"].median()
           .sort_values(ascending=False).index.tolist())
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.boxplot(data=df, x="APPT_CATEGORY", y="COMPRATE",
            order=order, palette=PALETTE, fliersize=2, ax=ax)
ax.set_title("Compensation by Appointment Category", fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("Appointment Category")
ax.set_ylabel("Annual Compensation (USD)")
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(plt.matplotlib.ticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"${x/1000:.0f}K"))
plt.xticks(rotation=20)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Interpretation. A clean ordering emerges: **Exe (Executive) > LS (Legal Service)

MSS (Management Supervisory) > ExS (Excepted Service) > Acting ≈ Ed (Educational) > CS (Career Service)**. The Executive category has the smallest headcount but the highest floor — every Executive earns more than the median CS employee. The Career Service category, despite its low median, has the widest right tail with outliers reaching nearly $400K — almost certainly senior agency directors who entered the system before the Executive appointment type was widely used.

7.4 · Bivariate — Tenure vs. Compensation¶

Intent: Test whether longer tenure systematically predicts higher pay. We use a downsampled scatter (8,000 points) plus a regression line plus the Pearson correlation, which together give us both a visual sense of dispersion and a single-number summary.

In [19]:
sample = df.sample(min(8000, len(df)), random_state=42)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.regplot(data=sample, x="TENURE_YEARS", y="COMPRATE",
            scatter_kws={"alpha": 0.18, "s": 18, "color": PALETTE[1]},
            line_kws={"color": PALETTE[4], "lw": 2.5}, ax=ax)
ax.set_title("Tenure vs. Annual Compensation", fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("Years of Tenure (as of Apr 2026)")
ax.set_ylabel("Annual Compensation (USD)")
ax.set_ylim(0, 350_000)
ax.set_xlim(0, df["TENURE_YEARS"].quantile(0.99))
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(plt.matplotlib.ticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"${x/1000:.0f}K"))
r = df[["TENURE_YEARS", "COMPRATE"]].corr().iloc[0, 1]
ax.text(0.02, 0.95, f"Pearson r = {r:.3f}", transform=ax.transAxes,
        fontsize=12, va="top",
        bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.4", fc="white", ec=PALETTE[0], lw=1.5))
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Interpretation. Pay rises with tenure, but the relationship is modest: Pearson r = 0.293, which means tenure explains only about 8.6% of the variance in compensation (r²). The visual confirms this — at every tenure level there is enormous spread, suggesting tenure is one lever among many (grade, agency, appointment type all matter independently). The horizontal banding visible in the cloud is the imprint of the GS-style grade structure: employees at the same grade earn very similar amounts regardless of which year they were hired.

7.5 · Multivariate — Heatmap of Median Pay (Agency × Appointment)¶

Intent: Cross-tabulate the two strongest grouping variables to see where in the agency × appointment matrix the highest pay concentrates.

In [20]:
top_agencies_15 = df["DESCRSHORT"].value_counts().head(15).index.tolist()
heat = (df[df["DESCRSHORT"].isin(top_agencies_15)]
        .groupby(["DESCRSHORT", "APPT_CATEGORY"])["COMPRATE"]
        .median().unstack())
heat = heat.loc[heat.mean(axis=1).sort_values(ascending=False).index]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 7.5))
sns.heatmap(heat / 1000, annot=True, fmt=".0f", cmap="YlGnBu",
            cbar_kws={"label": "Median Pay ($K)"},
            linewidths=0.5, linecolor="white", ax=ax)
ax.set_title("Median Compensation: Top 15 Agencies × Appointment Category",
             fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("Appointment Category")
ax.set_ylabel("Agency")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Interpretation. Three patterns jump out. First, agency baseline matters: the OCFO (Office of the Chief Financial Officer) Career Service medians ($119K) are higher than CS medians at almost every other agency, reflecting the technical/financial skill premium. Second, executive bands are wide across agencies — MPD's Exe-tier pay tops $300K, more than DCPS's executive pay ($125K), suggesting differing executive structures by mission. Third, the matrix has structural empty cells: not every agency uses every appointment category (UDC has no Exe column, DCPL has no Ed column), which is itself information about how each agency is organized.

7.6 · Multivariate — Compensation Density by Category and Status¶

Intent: Use a FacetGrid to see whether Term and Temp employees within each appointment category earn meaningfully less than their Regular counterparts — a question about whether appointment status creates a pay penalty independent of role.

In [21]:
top_cats = df["APPT_CATEGORY"].value_counts().head(4).index.tolist()
sub = df[df["APPT_CATEGORY"].isin(top_cats)].copy()
sub["APPT_STATUS_GROUP"] = sub["APPT_STATUS"].where(
    sub["APPT_STATUS"].isin(["Reg", "Term", "Temp"]), "Other"
)
g = sns.FacetGrid(sub, col="APPT_CATEGORY", col_order=top_cats,
                  hue="APPT_STATUS_GROUP", height=4, aspect=1.05,
                  hue_order=["Reg", "Term", "Temp", "Other"],
                  palette=PALETTE[:4])
g.map_dataframe(sns.kdeplot, x="COMPRATE", fill=True, alpha=0.45, common_norm=False)
g.add_legend(title="Status")
for ax in g.axes.flat:
    ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(plt.matplotlib.ticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"${x/1000:.0f}K"))
    ax.set_xlim(0, 250_000)
g.set_axis_labels("Annual Compensation", "Density")
g.set_titles(col_template="{col_name}")
g.fig.suptitle("Compensation Density by Appointment Category and Status",
               fontweight="bold", y=1.04)
plt.show()

Interpretation. Within each category, Temp employees earn meaningfully less than Regular employees — the green Temp curve is shifted left relative to the dark-blue Reg curve in CS, Ed, and MSS. Term employees are heterogeneous: in MSS the Term curve has a dramatic spike near $80K (likely a uniform-pay temporary classification), while in Ed it overlaps almost completely with Regular. The LS facet stands apart entirely — Legal Service medians sit so much higher than the other three categories that even its Term distribution exceeds the Regular distribution of the others.

7.7 · Interactive — Treemap of the Workforce¶

Intent: Provide an interactive overview where size encodes headcount and color encodes median pay, so the viewer can compare agencies on two dimensions at once and drill in via hover. This plot is interactive in the live notebook (HTML output).

In [22]:
treemap_df = (df.groupby(["APPT_CATEGORY", "DESCRSHORT"])
                .agg(employees=("COMPRATE", "size"),
                     median_pay=("COMPRATE", "median"))
                .reset_index())
fig = px.treemap(
    treemap_df,
    path=[px.Constant("DC Government"), "APPT_CATEGORY", "DESCRSHORT"],
    values="employees",
    color="median_pay",
    color_continuous_scale="Viridis",
    hover_data={"median_pay": ":$,.0f", "employees": ":,"},
    title="DC Workforce — Size by Headcount, Color by Median Pay (hover to drill in)",
)
fig.update_layout(margin=dict(t=60, l=10, r=10, b=10), height=650)
fig.show()

Interpretation. The treemap makes the structural shape of DC government immediately legible. The CS branch is the largest tile by far — it is the structural core of the government (~20K employees). Inside CS, DCPS dominates and shows a moderate (yellow-green) median pay. The smaller but high-paid Exe and LS branches are visually compact but bright, signaling that those sub-tree employees command much higher pay despite being a small share of headcount. Hover-tooltips reveal exact medians and headcounts for every (category, agency) cell.

7.8 · Surprise Plot — Agency Landscape Bubble Chart¶

Intent: Encode four dimensions in a single chart — agency size (x, log-scale), median pay (y), mean tenure (bubble size), and proportion of employees on Term appointments (color) — so we can see where each agency sits in the broader workforce landscape.

In [23]:
top25 = df["DESCRSHORT"].value_counts().head(25).index
bubble = (df[df["DESCRSHORT"].isin(top25)]
          .groupby("DESCRSHORT")
          .agg(median_pay=("COMPRATE", "median"),
               employees=("COMPRATE", "size"),
               mean_tenure=("TENURE_YEARS", "mean"),
               pct_term=("APPT_STATUS", lambda s: (s == "Term").mean() * 100))
          .reset_index())

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 7))
sc = ax.scatter(bubble["employees"], bubble["median_pay"],
                s=bubble["mean_tenure"] * 35,
                c=bubble["pct_term"], cmap="plasma",
                alpha=0.75, edgecolor="black", linewidth=0.6)
for _, row in bubble.iterrows():
    ax.annotate(row["DESCRSHORT"], (row["employees"], row["median_pay"]),
                fontsize=8.5, ha="center", va="center", color="white", weight="bold")
ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("Employees (log scale)")
ax.set_ylabel("Median Pay (USD)")
ax.set_title("Agency Landscape — Size, Pay, Tenure, and % on Term Appointments",
             fontweight="bold")
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(plt.matplotlib.ticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"${x/1000:.0f}K"))
cbar = plt.colorbar(sc, ax=ax, pad=0.01)
cbar.set_label("% on Term Appointments")
for tenure, label in [(5, "5 yrs"), (10, "10 yrs"), (20, "20 yrs")]:
    ax.scatter([], [], s=tenure*35, c="gray", alpha=0.5, edgecolor="black", label=label)
ax.legend(title="Mean Tenure", loc="upper left", framealpha=0.9, fontsize=10)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Interpretation. This single chart surfaces an insight none of the others can: agencies with high Term-employee shares (yellow / bright bubbles) are concentrated in the lower-pay band (DOES at ~50% Term, DOH at ~35% Term, both with medians under $95K). Meanwhile, the largest agencies (DCPS, MPD) sit at low-Term, mid-pay, with mature tenure profiles — they are the stable backbone of DC government. Small high-pay agencies like the OCFO and OAG cluster in the upper-left: highly compensated, lower-tenure, and almost entirely on Regular appointments.

8 · Additional Analysis / Findings¶

Two analyses extend beyond the primary research questions:

  1. Pay Dispersion Index — the P90/P10 ratio per agency, a standard inequality metric.
  2. Tenure-Controlled Pay Curve — median pay by tenure band within appointment category, isolating the tenure-pay slope from the appointment-mix confound visible in §7.4.

8.1 · Pay Dispersion (P90 ÷ P10) by Agency¶

Motivation. A single-number measure of internal pay inequality. A ratio of 2.0× means the top 10% of an agency earns at least twice what the bottom 10% earns. We compare the top-15 agencies and benchmark against the median ratio.

In [24]:
disp = (df[df["DESCRSHORT"].isin(top_agencies_15)]
        .groupby("DESCRSHORT")["COMPRATE"]
        .agg(p10=lambda x: x.quantile(0.10),
             p50="median",
             p90=lambda x: x.quantile(0.90))
        .assign(ratio=lambda x: x["p90"] / x["p10"])
        .sort_values("ratio", ascending=False))
disp.round(2)
Out[24]:
p10 p50 p90 ratio
DESCRSHORT
DDOT 22963.00 83500.00 134126.98 5.84
DCPS 46175.00 105071.00 137632.00 2.98
OCFO 70919.00 118558.00 190823.00 2.69
OAG 70102.00 118558.00 187672.00 2.68
DOH 52909.00 101290.00 137561.00 2.60
DPR 45760.00 68116.00 100827.00 2.20
DBH 63841.50 101290.00 137561.00 2.15
FEMS 64105.00 92113.00 134509.00 2.10
UDC 62750.11 81206.16 130737.00 2.08
DOES 64144.00 88300.00 129630.97 2.02
DCPL 57925.60 70919.00 116557.10 2.01
MPD 75433.00 110595.00 138250.00 1.83
DHS 68116.00 89329.00 119916.00 1.76
CFSA 76431.70 107046.00 130717.00 1.71
DOC 56617.00 73954.00 96799.55 1.71
In [25]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6.5))
bars = ax.barh(disp.index[::-1], disp["ratio"].values[::-1],
               color=PALETTE[5], edgecolor="white")
ax.set_title("Pay Dispersion (P90 ÷ P10) — Top 15 Agencies", fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("Ratio of 90th to 10th Percentile Pay")
for bar, v in zip(bars, disp["ratio"].values[::-1]):
    ax.text(bar.get_width() + 0.05, bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()/2,
            f"{v:.1f}×", va="center", fontsize=10)
ax.axvline(disp["ratio"].median(), color=PALETTE[4], ls="--", lw=1.5,
           label=f"Median  {disp['ratio'].median():.1f}×")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Interpretation. Pay dispersion is highest at DDOT (5.8×, the District Department of Transportation) — a striking outlier. DDOT mixes professional engineers and senior planners with a large field crew of laborers, and the ratio captures that two-tier structure. DCPS (3.0×) and OCFO (2.7×) follow, both bridging large entry-level workforces with senior specialists. By contrast, DOC (Department of Corrections, 1.7×) and CFSA (Child and Family Services, 1.7×) are the most internally compressed — both are agencies where the job market is dominated by a single skilled-but-not-executive role profile.

8.2 · Tenure-Controlled Pay Curve by Appointment Category¶

Motivation. Section 7.4 showed r = 0.293 globally — but that pools four very different appointment categories. By computing median pay within tenure bands separately for each category, we can see which pathways reward longevity and which plateau early.

In [26]:
df["TENURE_BIN"] = pd.cut(df["TENURE_YEARS"],
                          bins=[-0.1, 2, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 50],
                          labels=["0-2", "2-5", "5-10", "10-15",
                                  "15-20", "20-30", "30+"])
curve = (df[df["APPT_CATEGORY"].isin(top_cats)]
         .groupby(["TENURE_BIN", "APPT_CATEGORY"], observed=True)["COMPRATE"]
         .median().reset_index())
curve.head(10)
Out[26]:
TENURE_BIN APPT_CATEGORY COMPRATE
0 0-2 CS 71578.815
1 0-2 Ed 72655.000
2 0-2 LS 123522.000
3 0-2 MSS 124071.000
4 2-5 CS 79971.000
5 2-5 Ed 85361.000
6 2-5 LS 135017.000
7 2-5 MSS 122764.000
8 5-10 CS 92656.000
9 5-10 Ed 111403.000
In [27]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 6))
for i, cat in enumerate(top_cats):
    sub_c = curve[curve["APPT_CATEGORY"] == cat]
    ax.plot(sub_c["TENURE_BIN"].astype(str), sub_c["COMPRATE"],
            marker="o", lw=2.5, markersize=9, color=PALETTE[i], label=cat)
ax.set_title("Median Compensation by Tenure Band — Top Appointment Categories",
             fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("Years of Tenure")
ax.set_ylabel("Median Annual Compensation (USD)")
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(plt.matplotlib.ticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"${x/1000:.0f}K"))
ax.legend(title="Appointment", frameon=True)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Interpretation. The four categories follow visibly different career-arc shapes. LS (Legal Service) has the steepest slope by far: from $125K at 0–2 years to over $200K past 30 years — a 60% lifetime-pay gain. CS rises smoothly from $72K to a peak of $117K at 20–30 years, then dips slightly at 30+ (likely due to small sample at the very top of the tenure distribution and the survivorship of older lower-graded employees). MSS is flat from year one — it enters at ~$125K and stays there, indicating that Management/Supervisory appointment is a level hire, not a ladder. Ed (Educational) sits between CS and MSS — modest growth from $72K to $130K, then a slight decline.

9 · Insights and Conclusion¶

Did the research questions get answered?¶

Q1 — How does compensation vary across DC's agencies, and which agencies offer the highest median pay? Among the largest 15 agencies, the OCFO has the highest median pay at $118,558, followed by MPD ($110,595) and DCPS ($105,071). The lowest medians among the top-15 are DPR ($68,116) and DCPL ($70,919). Pay-by-agency is heavily mediated by appointment mix: agencies with many Executive positions (FEMS, OCFO) sit at the top of the cross-tabulated heatmap.

Q2 — Does tenure predict pay, and how does it differ across appointment categories? Tenure does predict pay (Pearson r = 0.293), but the global correlation hides a much richer picture. Inside the LS (Legal Service) track, tenure roughly doubles median compensation between entry and 30+ years. Inside MSS, tenure has almost no effect — pay is set on entry. CS and Ed both rise but plateau in the $110K–$130K range.

Q3 — Where is pay dispersion most extreme? DDOT has the most internally unequal pay distribution among large agencies (P90/P10 = 5.8×), almost twice the median ratio (2.1×). DCPS, OCFO, and OAG follow. The most compressed agencies are DOC, CFSA, and DHS.

Most significant patterns and trends¶

  • DC's public workforce is larger, more compressed, and more education-dominated than many would guess: DCPS alone is 28% of the cleaned dataset.
  • The compensation distribution is near-symmetric around $100K with a contained right tail — evidence of a strong grade-cap regime.
  • Appointment category is the dominant axis of pay variation; agency baseline is a secondary but real effect.
  • Term and Temp appointment statuses carry visible pay penalties within every category we examined.

Limitations¶

  1. The dataset captures a single point-in-time snapshot — we cannot observe individual pay trajectories, only cross-sectional differences.
  2. COMPRATE represents annual rate; we have no direct measure of hours actually worked. Some part-time and seasonal employees may be normalized to annual figures incorrectly.
  3. The 4,984 rows we excluded with COMPRATE < $10K may contain legitimate part-time positions that we filter out alongside the data-entry errors.
  4. We have no demographic information (race, gender, age), so we cannot examine pay equity along those dimensions — a major and well-documented question this dataset alone cannot answer.

Future directions¶

  • Longitudinal analysis — joining annual snapshots across multiple years to model individual pay growth.
  • Geocoded analysis — pairing agency data with DC ward boundaries to see how public-sector compensation flows geographically through the District.
  • Skill-premium decomposition — using the JOBTITLE field with NLP techniques to identify which competencies (cybersecurity, healthcare, law) command pay premiums above their grade.

10 · References¶

  4. Seaborn Documentation. Waskom, M. https://seaborn.pydata.org/
